name: Clean
summary: Functional language (since the 1990s) with uniqueness types: *T means no other reference exists, destructive update is safe. Semantically distinct from linearity — Clean is about graphs, linearity about lambda terms; uniqueness is about the past, linearity about the future. The earliest production language to put a static aliasing discipline at the type-system level for the purpose of safe in-place mutation in a pure functional setting.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Clean uniqueness types** (since the 1990s) use `*T` for "no other reference exists, destructive update is safe." The annotation guarantees the type checker that a value is unaliased at the moment of use, so an in-place mutation does not violate referential transparency.

Semantically distinct from linearity (Clean is about graphs, linearity about lambda terms; uniqueness is about the past, linearity about the future). Linear types restrict how a value *will* be used downstream; uniqueness types describe how a value *has* been used upstream. The two disciplines coincide on simple programs but diverge on graph-shaped data.

sections.title: Uniqueness vs linearity
sections.content:
Uniqueness types attach to *types*; linear types attach to *arrows* or to *binders*. A `*T` in Clean is a type-level statement about the current reference graph: there is no other reference to this value right now. A linear binder, by contrast, is a statement about the future: this value will be used exactly once.

The practical consequence: uniqueness composes naturally with destructive update because the absence of aliases is exactly what destructive update requires. Linearity composes naturally with resource APIs because exactly-once use models opening and closing a handle. Clean's design picks the discipline appropriate for in-place mutation in a functional language; linear types as in Linear Haskell pick the discipline appropriate for general resource control.

sections.title: Historical significance
sections.content: Clean is the earliest production language to put a static aliasing discipline at the type-system level for the purpose of safe in-place mutation in a pure functional setting. The uniqueness annotation `*World` threading through I/O — a *World value is consumed by every I/O action and a fresh one returned — is the historical predecessor of the explicit-state-passing style that survives today in Haskell's `IO` monad and in algebraic-effect systems' world-passing semantics.
